solution:
From the above figure, it is clear that, IgRg=(I- Ig)S
S ≃10-3×252⇒S ≃12.5 ×10-3
⇒1.25 ×10-2Ω

subject: Physics
q: A teacher in his physics laboratory allotted an experiment to determine the resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>G</mi></mfenced></math> a galvanometer. Students took the observations for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> deflection in the galvanometer. Which of the below is true for measuring value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi></math>?
question: A teacher in his physics laboratory allotted an experiment to determine the resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>G</mi></mfenced></math> a galvanometer. Students took the observations for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> deflection in the galvanometer. Which of the below is true for measuring value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi></math>?
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> deflection method cannot be used for determining the resistance of the galvanometer.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> deflection method can be used and in this case the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi></math> equals to twice the value of shunt resistance(s).

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> deflection method can be used and in this case, the G equals to three times the value of shunt resistance(s).

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> deflection method can be used and in this case the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi></math> value equals to the shunt resistance(s)
solution:
If a large resistance R is connected with a battery, then current will be approximately εR, and t will remain almost constant.

In 1st  case, only G is connected ⇒ig=i

In 2nd  case,

igR0=I-igS⇒ig2=SRg+Si

If ig2=13ig,⇒SRg+Si=13i

Rg=2S

subject: Physics
q: Two identical cells each of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> are connected in series across a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistance. An ideal voltmeter connected across <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistance reads <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The internal resistance of each cell is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>.
question: Two identical cells each of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> are connected in series across a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistance. An ideal voltmeter connected across <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistance reads <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The internal resistance of each cell is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>.
solution:
Let R1=10 Ω.

Using V=iR

i=310+2r

The voltmeter reading is V'=1.5 V.

Thus,
iR1=V'⇒10×310+2r=1.5
⇒20=10+2 r
⇒2r=10
⇒r=5 Ω

It is employed to measure the e.m.f of a battery whose internal resistance is $0.5 \Omega$. If the balance point is obtained at $\mathrm{l}=30$ $\mathrm{cm}$ from the positive end, the e.m.f. of the battery is
question: The length of a wire of a potentiometer is $100 \mathrm{~cm}$, and the e.m.f. of its standard cell is $\mathrm{E}$ volt. It is employed to measure the e.m.f of a battery whose internal resistance is $0.5 \Omega$. If the balance point is obtained at $\mathrm{l}=30$ $\mathrm{cm}$ from the positive end, the e.m.f. of the battery is
options: $\frac{30(\mathrm{E}-0.5 \mathrm{i})}{100}$, where $\mathrm{i}$ is the current in the potentiometer wire
solution:
Potential $\propto \mathrm{R}$
$\mathrm{R} \propto$ length $\Rightarrow$ Potential difference $\propto l$

subject: Physics
q: For the two cells having same EMF $E$ and internal resistance $r$, the current passing through the external resistor $6 \Omega$ is same when both the cells are connected either in parallel or in series. The value of internal resistance $r$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
question: For the two cells having same EMF $E$ and internal resistance $r$, the current passing through the external resistor $6 \Omega$ is same when both the cells are connected either in parallel or in series. The value of internal resistance $r$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
solution:
For series connection of two cells: $I_s = \frac{2E}{2r + 6}$.
For parallel connection: $I_p = \frac{E}{r/2 + 6} = \frac{2E}{r + 12}$.
Given $I_s = I_p$: $\frac{2E}{2r + 6} = \frac{2E}{r + 12}$.
This gives $2r + 6 = r + 12$, so $r = 6\Omega$.

subject: Physics
q: In the given figure, there is a circuit of potentiometer of length AB=10 m. The resistance per unit length is 0.1 Ω per cm. Across AB, a battery of emf E and internal resistance r is connected. The maximum value of emf measured by this potentiometer is:
question: In the given figure, there is a circuit of potentiometer of length AB=10 m. The resistance per unit length is 0.1 Ω per cm. Across AB, a battery of emf E and internal resistance r is connected. The maximum value of emf measured by this potentiometer is:
solution:
Maximum voltage that can be measured by this potentiometer will be equal to potential drop across AB, 

RAB=10×0.1×100=100 ohm

∴ VAB=620+100×100=6×100120=5 V

subject: Physics
q: In the figure shown below, a resistance of $150.4 \Omega$ is connected in series to an ammeter A of resistance $240 \Omega$. A shunt resistance of $10 \Omega$ is connected in parallel with the ammeter. The reading of the ammeter is _____ mA.
question: In the figure shown below, a resistance of $150.4 \Omega$ is connected in series to an ammeter A of resistance $240 \Omega$. A shunt resistance of $10 \Omega$ is connected in parallel with the ammeter. The reading of the ammeter is _____ mA.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{R}_2 \\ & \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}=150.4+\frac{240 \times 10}{250} \\ & =150.4+9.6=160 \Omega \\ & \mathrm{I}_1=\frac{\mathrm{I} \mathrm{R}_2}{240} \\ & \mathrm{I}_1=\frac{\mathrm{I} \times 9.6}{240} \\ & =\frac{20}{160} \times \frac{9.6}{2400}=\frac{1}{200}=5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}=5 \mathrm{~mA}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The current density in a cylindrical wire of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The current through the outer portion of the wire between radial distances <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>.
question: The current density in a cylindrical wire of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The current through the outer portion of the wire between radial distances <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>.
solution:
Current density is defined as current per unit cross-section area. Therefore,

i=JA=J×πR2-R24

⇒i=4×106×π×16×10-6×34

⇒i=48π A

subject: Physics
q: A voltmeter with internal resistance of $x\ \Omega$ can be used to measure upto $20$ V. In order to increase its measuring range to $30$ V, the required modification is to _____.
question: A voltmeter with internal resistance of $x\ \Omega$ can be used to measure upto $20$ V. In order to increase its measuring range to $30$ V, the required modification is to _____.
options: connect resistor of $\dfrac{x}{2}\ \Omega$, in series with voltmeter.
connect resistor of $\dfrac{x}{2}\ \Omega$, in parallel to voltmeter.
connect a resistor of $x\ \Omega$ in series with voltmeter.
connect resistor of $2x\ \Omega$ in parallel to voltmeter.
solution:
Let the maximum current for full-scale deflection of the voltmeter be $I_g$.

$I_g = \dfrac{V_1}{R_v} = \dfrac{20}{x}$

To increase the measuring range to $V_2 = 30$ V, a resistance $R$ must be connected in series with the voltmeter.

$V_2 = I_g(R_v + R)$

$30 = \dfrac{20}{x}(x + R)$

$30x = 20x + 20R$

$10x = 20R$

$R = \dfrac{x}{2}\ \Omega$

Thus, a resistor of $\dfrac{x}{2}\ \Omega$ should be connected in series with the voltmeter.

Answer: connect resistor of $\dfrac{x}{2}\ \Omega$, in series with voltmeter.

C.S. =θIg

Ig=θC.S.=50div2div/mA=25 mA

Potential difference =50mV

Resistance =VI=50mV25 mA=2Ω

solution:
ab=l100-l     

a+b=1000

b a = l−10 110−l

l 2 −10l=11000+ l 2

200l=11000

l= 110 2 =55

subject: Physics
q: In a metallic conductor, under the effect of applied electric field, the free electrons of the conductor
question: In a metallic conductor, under the effect of applied electric field, the free electrons of the conductor
options: Drift from higher potential to lower potential
Move with the uniform velocity throughout from lower potential to higher potential
Move in the straight line paths in the same direction
Move in the curved paths from lower potential to higher potential
solution:
The electrons inside the conductor move with an average velocity due to the applied field in a direction opposite to the field, this velocity is known as drift velocity. Electric field are applied from high to low potential region of the conductor. Also, electrons are negatively charged.

Electron drifts from lower potential to higher potential on curved path.

subject: Physics
q: A shunt of resistance $1 ~\Omega$ is connected across a galvanometer of $120 ~\Omega$ resistance. A current of $5.5$ ampere gives full scale deflection in the galvanometer. The current that will give full scale deflection in the absence of the shunt is nearly :
question: A shunt of resistance $1 ~\Omega$ is connected across a galvanometer of $120 ~\Omega$ resistance. A current of $5.5$ ampere gives full scale deflection in the galvanometer. The current that will give full scale deflection in the absence of the shunt is nearly :
solution:
The current that will given full scale deflection in the absence of the shunt is nearly equal to the current through the galvanometer when shunt is connected i.e. $\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}}$
As $I_g=\frac{I S}{G+S}$
$=\frac{5.5 \times 1}{120+1}=0.045$ ampere

∴10=n×1.6×10-19×5×10-6×2×10-3

⇒n=0.625×1028=625×1025

subject: Physics
q: An electric power line having total resistance of $2 \Omega$, delivers 1 kW of power at 250 V. The percentage efficiency of transmission line is $\_\_\_\_$ .
question: An electric power line having total resistance of $2 \Omega$, delivers 1 kW of power at 250 V. The percentage efficiency of transmission line is $\_\_\_\_$ .
solution:
Current in transmission line: $I = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{1000}{250} = 4$ A.
Power loss: $P_{loss} = I^2 R = 4^2 \times 2 = 32$ W.
Efficiency: $\eta = \frac{P - P_{loss}}{P} \times 100\%$
$= \frac{1000 - 32}{1000} \times 100\%$
$= 96.8\%$ which rounds to 96.9%.

subject: Physics
q: Under steady state condition the potential difference across the capacitor in the circuit is _______ V.
question: Under steady state condition the potential difference across the capacitor in the circuit is _______ V.
solution:
In steady state, the capacitor acts as an open circuit, meaning no current flows through the middle branch.

The circuit simplifies to a single loop containing the $2 \text{ V}$ battery, the $6 \Omega$ resistor, and the $2 \Omega$ resistor.

The total resistance of this loop is $R_{\text{eq}} = 2 + 6 = 8 \Omega$.

The current flowing through the loop is $I = \dfrac{2}{8} = 0.25 \text{ A}$.

The potential difference across the parallel branches is equal to the voltage drop across the $2 \Omega$ resistor:
$V = I \times 2 = 0.25 \times 2 = 0.5 \text{ V}$

Since there is no current in the middle branch, the voltage drop across the $4 \Omega$ resistor is zero. Thus, the entire potential difference of $0.5 \text{ V}$ appears across the capacitor.

Answer: $0.5$

subject: Physics
q: The thermo e.m.f. of a thermo-couple is $25 \mu \mathrm{V} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ at room temperature. A galvanometer of 40 ohm resistance, capable of detecting current as low as $10^{-5} \mathrm{~A}$, is connected with the thermo couple. The smallest temperature difference that can be detected by this sytem is
question: The thermo e.m.f. of a thermo-couple is $25 \mu \mathrm{V} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ at room temperature. A galvanometer of 40 ohm resistance, capable of detecting current as low as $10^{-5} \mathrm{~A}$, is connected with the thermo couple. The smallest temperature difference that can be detected by this sytem is
solution: $\Delta \mathrm{T}=\frac{40}{25 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-5}} \Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{T}=16^0 \mathrm{C}$

⇒q=t3+t412

⇒q=22 C

q: Two identical cells each of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> are connected in parallel across a parallel combination of two resistors each of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. A voltmeter connected in the circuit measures <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The internal resistance of each cell is :
question: Two identical cells each of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> are connected in parallel across a parallel combination of two resistors each of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. A voltmeter connected in the circuit measures <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The internal resistance of each cell is :
solution:
Equivalent resistance of the parallel 20 Ω branches will be 10 Ω.

Equivalent EMF of the two branches which are in parallel will be, =1.5r+1.5r1r+1r=1.5 V and the equivalent internal resistance for same branches will be, r2.

For loop containing voltmeter and 10 Ω resistor, 1.2-10i=0 ⇒i=0.12 A.

Reading of voltmeter should be,

1.2=1.5-ir2 ⇒ir2=0.3 ⇒r=0.6i=0.60.12= 5 Ω

subject: Physics
q: Two known resistances of $R \Omega$ and $2 R \Omega$ and one unknown resistance $X \Omega$ are connected in a circuit as shown in the figure. If the equivalent resistance between points $A$ and $B$ in the circuit is $X \Omega$, then the value of $X$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
question: Two known resistances of $R \Omega$ and $2 R \Omega$ and one unknown resistance $X \Omega$ are connected in a circuit as shown in the figure. If the equivalent resistance between points $A$ and $B$ in the circuit is $X \Omega$, then the value of $X$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
solution:
The circuit has 2R and X in series (top branch) parallel to R (bottom branch).
The equivalent resistance is $R_{eq} = \frac{(2R + X) \cdot R}{3R + X} = X$.
Setting up the equation: $X(3R + X) = R(2R + X)$, which simplifies to $X^2 + 2RX - 2R^2 = 0$.
Using the quadratic formula: $X = \frac{-2R \pm 2R\sqrt{3}}{2} = R(-1 \pm \sqrt{3})$.
Since resistance must be positive, $X = (\sqrt{3} - 1)R$ Ω.

subject: Physics
q: A circuit to verify Ohm's law uses ammeter and voltmeter in series or parallel connected corrected correctly to the resistor. In the circuit :
question: A circuit to verify Ohm's law uses ammeter and voltmeter in series or parallel connected corrected correctly to the resistor. In the circuit :
options: ammeter is always used in parallel and voltmeter is series
Both ammeter and voltmeter must be connected in parallel
ammeter is always connected in series and voltmeter in parallel
Both ammeter and voltmeter must be connected in series
solution: Since the potential difference is measured across the two ends of the component, the voltmeter must always be connected in parallel to the components. An ammeter is an instrument which is used to measure the amount of current flowing in the circuit or through any component. It also consists of two terminals. The current is measured at a point. Therefore, the ammeter must be connected in series with the circuit components.Ohm’s law states that voltage (potential difference) across a resistance in a circuit is directly proportional to the current flowing through it.Therefore, in Ohm’s law voltmeter is connected in parallel to the resistor and ammeter is connected in series.

subject: Physics
q:
A galvanometer having a coil of resistance $30 \Omega$ need 20 mA of current for full-scale deflection. If a maximum current of 3 A is to be measured using this galvanometer, the resistance of the shunt to be added to the galvanometer should be $\frac{30}{X} \Omega$, where $X$ is
Options
question:
A galvanometer having a coil of resistance $30 \Omega$ need 20 mA of current for full-scale deflection. If a maximum current of 3 A is to be measured using this galvanometer, the resistance of the shunt to be added to the galvanometer should be $\frac{30}{X} \Omega$, where $X$ is
Options
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \left(I-I_g\right) R=I_g G \\ & (3-0.02) \times R=0.02 \times G \Rightarrow R=\frac{30}{149} \\ & \Rightarrow 149=\text { Required } X\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment of potentiometer for measuring the internal resistance of primary cell a balancing length $\ell$ is obtained on the potentiometer wire when the cell is open circuit. Now the cell is short circuited by a resistance $R$. If $R$ is to be equal to the internal resistance of the cell the balancing length on the potentiometer wire will be
question: In an experiment of potentiometer for measuring the internal resistance of primary cell a balancing length $\ell$ is obtained on the potentiometer wire when the cell is open circuit. Now the cell is short circuited by a resistance $R$. If $R$ is to be equal to the internal resistance of the cell the balancing length on the potentiometer wire will be
solution:
Balancing length $l$ will give emf of cell $\therefore E=K l$
Here $\mathrm{K}$ is potential gradient.
If the cell is short circuited by resistance 'R'
Let balancing length obtained be $l^{\prime}$ then
$$
\begin{aligned}
& V=k l^{\prime} \\
& r=\left(\frac{E-V}{V}\right) R \\
\Rightarrow & V=E-V \\
\Rightarrow & 2 V=E \\
\text { or, } & 2 K l^{\prime}=K l \\
\therefore \quad & l^{\prime}=\frac{l}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: Which of the four resistances $P, Q, R$ and $S$ generate the greatest amount of heat when a current flows from A to B ?
question: Which of the four resistances $P, Q, R$ and $S$ generate the greatest amount of heat when a current flows from A to B ?
solution: $$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{R}_1=\mathrm{P}+\mathrm{Q}=2 \Omega+4 \Omega=6 \Omega \\ & \mathrm{R}_2=\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{S}=1 \Omega+2 \Omega=3 \Omega \\ & \mathrm{I}_1 \mathrm{R}_1=\mathrm{I}_2 \mathrm{R}_2 \\ & \mathrm{I}_1=\frac{\mathrm{R}_2}{\mathrm{R}_1} \mathrm{I}_2=\frac{3}{6} \mathrm{I}_2=\frac{\mathrm{I}_2}{2} \end{aligned} $$ or $\mathrm{I}_2=2 \mathrm{I}_1$ Heat flow $\mathrm{H}=\mathrm{I}^2 \mathrm{Rt}$ For $\mathrm{Q}, \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{Q}}=\mathrm{I}_1^2 \mathrm{Qt}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_2^2}{4} \times 4 \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{I}_2^2 \mathrm{t}$ For $\mathrm{S}, \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{S}}=\mathrm{I}_2^2 \mathrm{St}=\mathrm{I}_2^2 \cdot 2 \mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{I}_2^2 \mathrm{t}$ $\therefore$ Greatest amount of heat generated by $\mathrm{S}$.

subject: Physics
q: The value of current in the 6 Ω resistance is:
question: The value of current in the 6 Ω resistance is:
solution:
Applying KCL at point P,

V-06+V-905+V-14020=0
⇒10 V+12 V-1080+3 V-420=0
⇒V=60
∴ Current in 6 Ω=V-06=10 A
Hence, option 3.

subject: Physics
q: Refer to the figure given below, current between terminals $A$ and $B$ is _____ A.
question: Refer to the figure given below, current between terminals $A$ and $B$ is _____ A.
solution:
The given circuit consists of four parallel branches connected between the left and right common vertical wires.

Let's find the equivalent electromotive force (EMF) and resistance for each branch:
For the top three active branches, each contains three $5\text{ V}$ cells and three $3\ \Omega$ resistors in series.
Equivalent EMF of each active branch, $E = 5 + 5 + 5 = 15\text{ V}$.
Equivalent resistance of each active branch, $R = 3 + 3 + 3 = 9\ \Omega$.

Since these three identical active branches are connected in parallel, we can replace them with a single equivalent voltage source:
Equivalent EMF, $E_{eq} = 15\text{ V}$ (as they are identical and in parallel).
Equivalent internal resistance, $R_{eq} = \dfrac{9}{3} = 3\ \Omega$.

The fourth (bottom) branch is connected between terminals $A$ and $B$ and acts as the load for this equivalent source. It contains three $3\ \Omega$ resistors in series.
Resistance of the branch $AB$, $R_{AB} = 3 + 3 + 3 = 9\ \Omega$.

Now, the circuit is simplified to an equivalent source of $15\text{ V}$ with internal resistance $3\ \Omega$ connected across a load of $9\ \Omega$.
The current $I$ flowing between terminals $A$ and $B$ (through the bottom branch) is given by Ohm's law:
$I = \dfrac{E_{eq}}{R_{eq} + R_{AB}}$

$I = \dfrac{15}{3 + 9} = \dfrac{15}{12}\text{ A}$

$I = 1.25\text{ A}$

Answer: $1.25$

subject: Physics
q:
This question contains Statement-1 and Statement-2. Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
Statement-1: The temperature dependence of resistance is usually given as $R=R_0(1+\alpha \Delta t)$. The resistance of a wire changes from $100 \Omega$ to $150 \Omega$ when its temperature is increased from $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $227^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. This implies that $\alpha=2.5 \times 10^{-3} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
Statement 2: $R=R_i(1+\alpha \Delta T)$ is valid only when the change in the temperature $\Delta T$ is small and $\Delta R=$ $\left(R-R_0\right) \ll R_0$.
question:
This question contains Statement-1 and Statement-2. Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
Statement-1: The temperature dependence of resistance is usually given as $R=R_0(1+\alpha \Delta t)$. The resistance of a wire changes from $100 \Omega$ to $150 \Omega$ when its temperature is increased from $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $227^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. This implies that $\alpha=2.5 \times 10^{-3} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
Statement 2: $R=R_i(1+\alpha \Delta T)$ is valid only when the change in the temperature $\Delta T$ is small and $\Delta R=$ $\left(R-R_0\right) \ll R_0$.
options: Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is false
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is the correct explanation of Statement-1.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is not the correct explanation of Statement-1.
Statement-1 is false, Statement-2 is true
solution: $$ \text { Directions: Question numbers } 12 \text { and } 13 \text { are based on the following paragraph. } $$ A current loop $A B C D$ is held fixed on the plane of the paper as shown in the figure. q: An aluminium wire is stretched to make its length, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>%</mo></math> larger. The percentage change in resistance is
question: An aluminium wire is stretched to make its length, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>%</mo></math> larger. The percentage change in resistance is
solution:
Volume of wire having cross-sectional area A and length l is, V=Al

Resistance of a wire is given by, R=ρlA=ρlVl=ρl2V

As change in length is small,

∆RR×100%=2∆ll×100%=2×0.4%=0.8%

subject: Physics
q: Two coils require <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> minutes and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn></math> minutes respectively to produce same amount of heat energy when connected separately to the same source. If they are connected in parallel arrangement to the same source; the time required to produce same amount of heat by the combination of coils, will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>min</mi></math>.
question: Two coils require <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> minutes and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn></math> minutes respectively to produce same amount of heat energy when connected separately to the same source. If they are connected in parallel arrangement to the same source; the time required to produce same amount of heat by the combination of coils, will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>min</mi></math>.
solution:
If H is the heat required to heat the water, then

P1×60=H⇒P1=H60 and P2×20=H⇒P2=H20

When the coils are connected in parallel their powers will get added. If the required time now is t, then

P1+P2t=H⇒t=HH60+H20=15 min

subject: Physics
q: A wire of resistance $20 \Omega$ is divided into 10 equal parts, resulting pairs. A combination of two parts are connected in parallel and so on. Now resulting pairs of parallel combination are connected in series. The equivalent resistance of final combination is _____ $\Omega$.
question: A wire of resistance $20 \Omega$ is divided into 10 equal parts, resulting pairs. A combination of two parts are connected in parallel and so on. Now resulting pairs of parallel combination are connected in series. The equivalent resistance of final combination is _____ $\Omega$.
solution: Each part has resistance $=2 \Omega$ 2 parts are connected in parallel so, $\mathrm{R}=1 \Omega$ Now, there will be 5 parts each of resistance $1 \Omega$, they are connected in series. $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}=5 \mathrm{R}, \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}=5 \Omega$
